lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Good Michael, look you to the guard to-night:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Let’s teach ourselves that honourable stop,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Not to outsport discretion.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Iago hath direction what to do;

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: But, notwithstanding, with my personal eye

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Will I look to’t.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Iago is most honest.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Michael, good night: to-morrow with your earliest

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Let me have speech with you. To Desdemona. Come, my dear love,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The purchase made, the fruits are to ensue;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That profit’s yet to come ’tween me and you.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Good night.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Welcome, Iago; we must to the watch.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Not this hour, lieutenant; ’tis not yet ten o’ the clock. Our

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: general cast us thus early for the love of his Desdemona; who

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: let us not therefore blame: he hath not yet made wanton the

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: night with her; and she is sport for Jove.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: She’s a most exquisite lady.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And, I’ll warrant her, full of game.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Indeed, she’s a most fresh and delicate creature.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What an eye she has! methinks it sounds a parley of provocation.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: An inviting eye; and yet methinks right modest.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And when she speaks, is it not an alarum to love?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: She is indeed perfection.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Well, happiness to their sheets! Come, lieutenant, I have a stoup of

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: wine; and here without are a brace of Cyprus gallants that would

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: fain have a measure to the health of black Othello.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Not to-night, good Iago: I have very poor and unhappy brains for

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: drinking: I could well wish courtesy would invent some other custom of

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: entertainment.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O, they are our friends; but one cup: I’ll drink for you.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I have drunk but one cup to-night, and that was craftily qualified

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: too, and, behold, what innovation it makes here: I am unfortunate in

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: the infirmity, and dare not task my weakness with any more.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What, man! ’tis a night of revels: the gallants desire it.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Where are they?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Here at the door; I pray you, call them in.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I’ll do’t; but it dislikes me.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: If I can fasten but one cup upon him,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: With that which he hath drunk to-night already,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He’ll be as full of quarrel and offence

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As my young mistress’ dog. Now, my sick fool Roderigo,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Whom love hath turn’d almost the wrong side out,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To Desdemona hath to-night caroused

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Potations pottle-deep; and he’s to watch:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Three lads of Cyprus, noble swelling spirits,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That hold their honours in a wary distance,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: The very elements of this warlike isle,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Have I to-night fluster’d with flowing cups,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And they watch too. Now, ’mongst this flock of drunkards,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Am I to put our Cassio in some action

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That may offend the isle.—But here they come:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: If consequence do but approve my dream,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: My boat sails freely, both with wind and stream.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: ’Fore God, they have given me a rouse already.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Good faith, a little one; not past a pint, as I am a soldier.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Some wine, ho! And let me the canakin clink, clink; And let

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: me the canakin clink A soldier’s a man; A life’s but a

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: span; Why, then, let a soldier drink. Some wine, boys!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: ’Fore God, an excellent song.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I learned it in England, where, indeed, they are most potent in

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: potting: your Dane, your German, and your swag-bellied Hollander—Drink, ho!—are nothing to

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: your English.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Is your Englishman so expert in his drinking?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Why, he drinks you, with facility, your Dane dead drunk; he sweats

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: not to overthrow your Almain; he gives your Hollander a vomit, ere

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: the next pottle can be filled.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: To the health of our general!

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: I am for it, lieutenant; and I’ll do you justice.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O sweet England!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: King Stephen was a worthy peer,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: His breeches cost him but a crown;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He held them sixpence all too dear,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: With that he call’d the tailor lown.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He was a wight of high renown,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And thou art but of low degree:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: ’Tis pride that pulls the country down;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Then take thine auld cloak about thee.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Some wine, ho!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Why, this is a more exquisite song than the other.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Will you hear’t again?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: No; for I hold him to be unworthy of his place that

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: does those things. Well, God’s above all; and there be souls must

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: be saved, and there be souls must not be saved.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: It’s true, good lieutenant.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: For mine own part—no offence to the general, nor any man of

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: quality—I hope to be saved.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And so do I too, lieutenant.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Ay, but, by your leave, not before me; the lieutenant is to

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: be saved before the ancient. Let’s have no more of this; let’s

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: to our affairs.—Forgive us our sins!—Gentlemen, let’s look to our business. Do

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: not think, gentlemen, I am drunk: this is my ancient; this is

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: my right hand, and this is my left: I am not drunk

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: now; I can stand well enough, and speak well enough.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Excellent well.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Why, very well then; you must not think then that I am drunk.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: To the platform, masters; come, let’s set the watch.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You see this fellow that is gone before;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He is a soldier fit to stand by Caesar

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And give direction: and do but see his vice;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: ’Tis to his virtue a just equinox,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: The one as long as the other: ’tis pity of him.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I fear the trust Othello puts him in.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: On some odd time of his infirmity,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Will shake this island.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: But is he often thus?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: ’Tis evermore the prologue to his sleep:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He’ll watch the horologe a double set,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: If drink rock not his cradle.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: It were well

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: The general were put in mind of it.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Perhaps he sees it not; or his good nature

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Prizes the virtue that appears in Cassio,

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: And looks not on his evils: is not this true?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: How now, Roderigo! I pray you, after the lieutenant; go.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: And ’tis great pity that the noble Moor

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Should hazard such a place as his own second

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: With one of an ingraft infirmity:

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: It were an honest action to say

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: So to the Moor.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Not I, for this fair island:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I do love Cassio well; and would do much

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To cure him of this evil—But, hark! what noise? Cry within: “Help! help!”

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: You rogue! you rascal!

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: What’s the matter, lieutenant?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: A knave teach me my duty!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I’ll beat the knave into a twiggen bottle.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Beat me!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Dost thou prate, rogue? Striking Roderigo.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Nay, good lieutenant; Staying him.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: I pray you, sir, hold your hand.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Let me go, sir,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Or I’ll knock you o’er the mazzard.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Come, come, you’re drunk.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Drunk! They fight.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Away, I say; go out, and cry a mutiny.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Nay, good lieutenant—alas, gentlemen;—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Help, ho!—Lieutenant—sir—Montano—sir;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Help, masters!—Here’s a goodly watch indeed!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Who’s that which rings the bell?—Diablo, ho!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: The town will rise: God’s will, lieutenant, hold!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You will be shamed for ever.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: What is the matter here?

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: ’Zounds, I bleed still; I am hurt to the death. Faints.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Hold, for your lives!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Hold, ho! Lieutenant—sir—Montano—gentlemen—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Have you forgot all sense of place and duty?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Hold! the general speaks to you; hold, hold, for shame!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Why, how now, ho! from whence ariseth this?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Are we turn’d Turks, and to ourselves do that

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Which heaven hath forbid the Ottomites?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For Christian shame, put by this barbarous brawl:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: He that stirs next to carve for his own rage

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Holds his soul light; he dies upon his motion.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Silence that dreadful bell: it frights the isle

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: From her propriety. What is the matter, masters?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Honest Iago, that look’st dead with grieving,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Speak, who began this? on thy love, I charge thee.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I do not know: friends all but now, even now,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: In quarter, and in terms like bride and groom

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Devesting them for bed; and then, but now—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As if some planet had unwitted men—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Swords out, and tilting one at other’s breast,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: In opposition bloody. I cannot speak

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Any beginning to this peevish odds;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And would in action glorious I had lost

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Those legs that brought me to a part of it!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: How comes it, Michael, you are thus forgot?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I pray you, pardon me; I cannot speak.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Worthy Montano, you were wont be civil;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The gravity and stillness of your youth

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The world hath noted, and your name is great

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: In mouths of wisest censure: what’s the matter,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That you unlace your reputation thus

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And spend your rich opinion for the name

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Of a night-brawler? give me answer to it.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Worthy Othello, I am hurt to danger:

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Your officer, Iago, can inform you—

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: While I spare speech, which something now

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: offends me—

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Of all that I do know: nor know I aught

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: By me that’s said or done amiss this night;

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Unless self-charity be sometimes a vice,

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: And to defend ourselves it be a sin

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: When violence assails us.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Now, by heaven,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: My blood begins my safer guides to rule;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And passion, having my best judgment collied,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Assays to lead the way: if I once stir,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Or do but lift this arm, the best of you

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Shall sink in my rebuke. Give me to know

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: How this foul rout began, who set it on;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And he that is approved in this offence,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Though he had twinn’d with me, both at a birth,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Shall lose me. What! in a town of war,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Yet wild, the people’s hearts brimful of fear,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To manage private and domestic quarrel,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: In night, and on the court and guard of safety!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: ’Tis monstrous. Iago, who began’t?

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: If partially affined, or leagued in office,

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Thou dost deliver more or less than truth,

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Thou art no soldier.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Touch me not so near:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I had rather have this tongue cut from my mouth

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Than it should do offence to Michael Cassio;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Yet, I persuade myself, to speak the truth

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Shall nothing wrong him. Thus it is, general.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Montano and myself being in speech,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: There comes a fellow crying out for help:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And Cassio following him with determined sword,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To execute upon him. Sir, this gentleman

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Steps in to Cassio, and entreats his pause:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Myself the crying fellow did pursue,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Lest by his clamour—as it so fell out—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: The town might fall in fright: he, swift of foot,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Outran my purpose; and I return’d the rather

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For that I heard the clink and fall of swords,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And Cassio high in oath; which till to-night

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I ne’er might say before. When I came back—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For this was brief—I found them close together,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: At blow and thrust; even as again they were

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: When you yourself did part them.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: More of this matter cannot I report:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But men are men; the best sometimes forget:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Though Cassio did some little wrong to him,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As men in rage strike those that wish them best,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Yet surely Cassio, I believe, received

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: From him that fled some strange indignity,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Which patience could not pass.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I know, Iago,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Thy honesty and love doth mince this matter,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Making it light to Cassio. Cassio, I love thee

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: But never more be officer of mine.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: What’s the matter?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: All’s well now, sweeting; come away to bed.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Sir, for your hurts, myself will be your surgeon:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Lead him off. To Montano, who is led off.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Iago, look with care about the town,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And silence those whom this vile brawl distracted.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Come, Desdemona: ’tis the soldiers’ life

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To have their balmy slumbers waked with strife.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What, are you hurt, lieutenant?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Ay, past all surgery.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Marry, heaven forbid!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Reputation, reputation, reputation! O, I have lost my reputation! I have lost

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: the immortal part of myself, and what remains is bestial. My reputation,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Iago, my reputation!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As I am an honest man, I thought you had received some

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: bodily wound; there is more sense in that than in reputation. Reputation

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: is an idle and most false imposition: oft got without merit, and

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: lost without deserving: you have lost no reputation at all, unless you

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: repute yourself such a loser. What, man! there are ways to recover

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: the general again: you are but now cast in his mood, a

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: punishment more in policy than in malice, even so as one would

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: beat his offenceless dog to affright an imperious lion: sue to him

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: again, and he’s yours.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I will rather sue to be despised than to deceive so good

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: a commander with so slight, so drunken, and so indiscreet an officer.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Drunk? and speak parrot? and squabble? swagger? swear? and discourse fustian with

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: one’s own shadow? O thou invisible spirit of wine, if thou hast

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: no name to be known by, let us call thee devil!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What was he that you followed with your sword? What had he done to you?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I know not.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Is’t possible?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I remember a mass of things, but nothing distinctly; a quarrel, but

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: nothing wherefore. O God, that men should put an enemy in their

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: mouths to steal away their brains! that we should, with joy, pleasance

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: revel and applause, transform ourselves into beasts!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Why, but you are now well enough: how came you thus recovered?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: It hath pleased the devil drunkenness to give place to the devil

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: wrath; one unperfectness shows me another, to make me frankly despise myself.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Come, you are too severe a moraler: as the time, the place,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: and the condition of this country stands, I could heartily wish this

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: had not befallen; but, since it is as it is, mend it

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: for your own good.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I will ask him for my place again; he shall tell me

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I am a drunkard! Had I as many mouths as Hydra, such

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: an answer would stop them all. To be now a sensible man,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: by and by a fool, and presently a beast! O strange! Every

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: inordinate cup is unblessed and the ingredient is a devil.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Come, come, good wine is a good familiar creature, if it be

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: well used: exclaim no more against it. And, good lieutenant, I think

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: you think I love you.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I have well approved it, sir. I drunk!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You or any man living may be drunk! at a time, man.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I’ll tell you what you shall do. Our general’s wife is now

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: the general: may say so in this respect, for that he hath

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: devoted and given up himself to the contemplation, mark, and denotement of

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: her parts and graces: confess yourself freely to her; importune her help

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: to put you in your place again: she is of so free,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: so kind, so apt, so blessed a disposition, she holds it a

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: vice in her goodness not to do more than she is requested:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: this broken joint between you and her husband entreat her to splinter;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: and, my fortunes against any lay worth naming, this crack of your

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: love shall grow stronger than it was before.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: You advise me well.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I protest, in the sincerity of love and honest kindness.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I think it freely; and betimes in the morning I will beseech

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: the virtuous Desdemona to undertake for me: I am desperate of my

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: fortunes if they cheque me here.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You are in the right. Good night, lieutenant; I must to the watch.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Good night, honest Iago.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And what’s he then that says I play the villain?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: When this advice is free I give and honest,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Probal to thinking and indeed the course

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To win the Moor again? For ’tis most easy

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: The inclining Desdemona to subdue

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: In any honest suit: she’s framed as fruitful

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As the free elements. And then for her

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To win the Moor—were’t to renounce his baptism,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: All seals and symbols of redeemed sin,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: His soul is so enfetter’d to her love,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That she may make, unmake, do what she list,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Even as her appetite shall play the god

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: With his weak function. How am I then a villain

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To counsel Cassio to this parallel course,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Directly to his good? Divinity of hell!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: When devils will the blackest sins put on,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: They do suggest at first with heavenly shows,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As I do now: for whiles this honest fool

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Plies Desdemona to repair his fortunes

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And she for him pleads strongly to the Moor,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I’ll pour this pestilence into his ear,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That she repeals him for her body’s lust;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And by how much she strives to do him good,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She shall undo her credit with the Moor.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: So will I turn her virtue into pitch,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And out of her own goodness make the net

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That shall enmesh them all.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I do follow here in the chase, not like a hound that

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: hunts, but one that fills up the cry. My money is almost

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: spent; I have been to-night exceedingly well cudgelled; and I think the

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: issue will be, I shall have so much experience for my pains,

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: and so, with no money at all and a little more wit,

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: return again to Venice.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: How poor are they that have not patience!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What wound did ever heal but by degrees?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Thou know’st we work by wit, and not by witchcraft;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And wit depends on dilatory time.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Does’t not go well? Cassio hath beaten thee.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And thou, by that small hurt, hast cashier’d Cassio:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Though other things grow fair against the sun,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Yet fruits that blossom first will first be ripe:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Content thyself awhile. By the mass, ’tis morning;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Pleasure and action make the hours seem short.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Retire thee; go where thou art billeted:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Away, I say; thou shalt know more hereafter:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Nay, get thee gone.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Two things are to be done:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: My wife must move for Cassio to her mistress;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I’ll set her on;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Myself the while to draw the Moor apart,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And bring him jump when he may Cassio find

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Soliciting his wife: ay, that’s the way

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Dull not device by coldness and delay.

stage_directions.text: Enter Othello, Desdemona, Cassio, and Attendants.
Reenter Cassio; with him Montano and Gentlemen; servants following with wine.
Look, if my gentle love be not raised up! I’ll make thee an example.
Exeunt all but Iago and Cassio.

glosses.definition: let revelry push out good judgment
a summons or call to love-making
the Danes — reputed expert drinkers in Elizabethan stereotype
exact balance; equal measure of good and bad
by God's wounds — a violent oath
a wicker-covered bottle — Cassio's insult, making Roderigo into a vessel to be beaten
cut and serve out his own fury; take violent liberty
on good terms; at peace with one another

glosses.phrase: in terms like bride and groom

glosses.definition: biased by loyalty; bound by kinship
the devil of drunkenness — Cassio's confession
the many-headed monster of Greek myth; here, drunkenness with countless mouths
set with a splint; mend as one would a broken limb
hellish theology! — Iago exults at his own perverse doctrine
tar; black resin — Iago's metaphor for how virtue will become corruption

glosses.phrase: jump when he may Cassio find
glosses.definition: manage the exact moment when he may come upon Cassio

annotations.title: Drinking as weaponized sociability
annotations.body:
Iago turns a celebration into a trap by deploying conviviality. He sings drinking songs (stock tavern ballads the audience would have recognized), invokes English and Danish drinking stereotypes, and plies Cassio with wine while ostensibly moderating his own intake. The sociable drinker is, for Iago, the most useful kind of person — someone who becomes legible at the same moment he becomes impaired.

Shakespeare is precise about the sequence. Cassio is baited, drunk, roused to quarrel, provoked into striking Montano, and ruined — all in approximately 200 lines of continuous stage time. The scene's pace is part of the argument: the machinery of social destruction is fast.
sources: Keith Wrightson, 'Alehouses, Order and Reformation in Rural England', in *Popular Culture and Class Conflict* (1981)
Peter Burke, *Popular Culture in Early Modern Europe* (1978)

annotations.title: Divinity of hell: Iago's theology of deception
annotations.body:
Iago's soliloquy at scene's end explains his method in theological terms. When devils work their blackest sins, they first tempt with heavenly appearances. Cassio, repentant, will petition Desdemona; Desdemona, generous, will plead his case; Othello, misreading generosity as entanglement, will convict them both. Every link in the chain is virtue turned into a weapon.

This inverted theology — 'Divinity of hell!' — is the closest the play comes to giving Iago a consistent doctrine. He does not disbelieve in goodness; he uses it. The distinction between tempter and temptation collapses. Desdemona's kindness will kill her not by being misrepresented but by being true.
sources: Robert B. Heilman, *Magic in the Web: Action and Language in Othello* (1956)
Susan Snyder, *The Comic Matrix of Shakespeare's Tragedies* (1979)

trials.prompt: Why does Cassio initially refuse to drink with Iago?
options: He has taken a religious oath against alcohol
He knows he has a weak head for drink and is on duty
He dislikes the taste of the local Cypriot wine
Othello has specifically forbidden it

trials.prompt: When Iago sings drinking songs and invokes English and Danish drinking stereotypes, what is his actual purpose?
options: Killing time until the next official event
Performing sociability as a tool — making Cassio feel peer pressure to drink past his limit
Demonstrating his knowledge of foreign cultures to impress Montano
Practicing songs for a future court performance

trials.prompt: What is the consequence of the drunken brawl?
options: Iago is publicly disgraced by Othello
Cassio is stripped of his lieutenant's rank
Montano is banished from Cyprus
Roderigo is expelled from the garrison

trials.prompt: In his closing soliloquy Iago explains that 'when devils will the blackest sins put on, / They do suggest at first with heavenly shows.' How will this doctrine play out in the next act?
options: Iago will openly tempt Othello with obvious lies
Iago will use Desdemona's genuine kindness to Cassio as the evidence of her adultery — turning virtue into weapon
Iago will disguise himself as a priest and hear confession
Iago will perform miracles to dazzle Othello

trials.prompt: What does Cassio lament losing more than anything else after his disgrace?
options: His lieutenant's commission and its salary
His reputation — the 'immortal part of myself'
The respect of Othello personally
His safe return passage to Venice
